title: Problem 3: Markov Chain with Interpolation Macdonald Stationary Distribution
author: Lauren Williams (Harvard University)
statement_excerpt: Let $\lambda=(\lambda_1 > \dots > \lambda_n \geq 0)$ be a partition with distinct parts. Assume moreover that $\lambda$ is {\it restricted}, in the sense that it has a unique part of size $0$ and no part of size $1$. Does there exist a nontrivial Markov chain on $S_n(\lambda)$ whose stationary distribution is given by $$\frac{F^*_{\mu}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q=1,t)}{P^*_{\lambda}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q=1,t)} \text{ for }\mu\in S_n(\lambda)$$ where $F^*_{\mu}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q,t)$ and $P^*_{\lambda}(x_1,\dots,x_n;q,t)$ are the interpolation ASEP polynomial and interpolation Macdonald polynomial, respectively? If so, prove that the Markov chain you construct has the desired stationary distribution. By ``nontrivial'' we mean that the transition probabilities of the Markov chain should not be described using the polynomials $F_{\mu}^*(x_1,\dots,x_n; q,t)$.
normalized_statement: Let $\lambda=(\lambda_1 > \dots > \lambda_n \geq 0)$ be a partition with distinct parts. Assume moreover that $\lambda$ is {\it restricted}, in the sense that it has a unique part of size $0$ and no part of size $1$. Does there exist a nontrivial Markov chain on $S_n(\lambda)$ whose stationary distribution is given by $$\frac{F^*_{\mu}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q=1,t)}{P^*_{\lambda}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q=1,t)} \text{ for }\mu\in S_n(\lambda)$$ where $F^*_{\mu}(x_1,\dots,x_n; q,t)$ and $P^*_{\lambda}(x_1,\dots,x_n;q,t)$ are the interpolation ASEP polynomial and interpolation Macdonald polynomial, respectively? If so, prove that the Markov chain you construct has the desired stationary distribution. By ``nontrivial'' we mean that the transition probabilities of the Markov chain should not be described using the polynomials $F_{\mu}^*(x_1,\dots,x_n; q,t)$.
definitions: Let $\lambda=(\lambda_1 > \dots > \lambda_n \geq 0)$ be a partition with distinct parts
quantifier_summary: Existential quantifier detected: the proof must construct or certify an object.

skill.name: math-research
skill.description: General mathematics research assistant. Use when working on math proofs, reading arxiv or journal papers, understanding theorems and definitions, identifying proof gaps, developing proof strategies, translating math to LaTeX, or connecting results across papers. Covers all areas: combinatorics, graph theory, spectral methods, probability, algebra, analysis, topology, and more.
